coredata.dc:title: Dynamic restructuring of carbon dots/copper oxide supported on mesoporous hydroxyapatite brings exceptional catalytic activity in the reduction of 4-nitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Qing
Xu, Wei
Li, Ning
Xue, Chaorui
Wang, Yanzhong
Li, Ying
Wang, Huiqi
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: A low-cost hybrid catalyst, carbon dots/copper oxide supported on mesoporous hydroxyapatite (CDs/CuO/mHA), was successfully prepared in this work. The resulting CDs/CuO/mHA showed excellent catalytic activity toward the reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) even under dark condition. It was demonstrated that the CuO phase underwent dynamic restructuring induced by the reactive environments and significantly boosted the electron transport, which fundamentally promoted the reduction of 4-NP. Introducing visible light illumination further resulted in an encouraging activity improvement owing to the acceleration of the restructuring of CuO by the photogenerated charges. In addition, CDs acting as electron donor, mHA providing porous structure and high specific surface area, as well as good interfacial bonding being the efficient charge transport channels, also played vital roles in improving the catalytic performance. The moles-normalized rate constant obtained for CDs/CuO/mHA was about 17 times higher than that obtained for CuO.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Copper oxide
Mesoporous
Hydroxyapatite
Restructuring
Catalytic activity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs/CuO/mHA composite
Catalytic activity of CDs/CuO/mHA in the reduction of 4-NP

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the synthetic process of CDs/CuO/mHA. (B) TEM image, (C) HRTEM image and (D–H) STEM and elemental mapping images of CDs/CuO/mHA.
(A) XRD patterns, (B) FTIR spectra, High-resolution XPS spectra of (C) Ca2p, (D) C1s, and (E) Cu2p of as synthesized samples.
(A) XRD patterns of CDs/CuO/mHA before and after reaction under dark condition. (B) XRD pattern of Cu2O (Inset: photographs of Cu2O before and after being added to 4-NP).

(A) Plots of Ct/C0 versus time for the 4-NP reduction over CDs/CuO/mHA in the presence of ethanol scavenger (illuminated with the visible light at 100 mW/cm2). (B) PL spectra of CDs in the presence of acceptor 2,4-dinitrotoluene and donor N,N-diethylaninline. (C) The photocurrent-time (I-t) curves and (D) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plots of as synthesized samples.
(A) Conversion rate of 4-NP over CDs/CuO/mHA under illumination with different wavelength. (B) Recyclability of CDs/CuO/mHA illuminated with the visible light at 100 mW/cm2.
Schematic mechanism for the catalytic reduction of 4-NP by CDs/CuO/mHA.

head.ce:title: Dynamic restructuring of carbon dots/copper oxide supported on mesoporous hydroxyapatite brings exceptional catalytic activity in the reduction of 4-nitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xueyuan Road 3, Taiyuan 030051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xueyuan Road 3, Taiyuan 030051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tsinghua University, State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tsinghua University, State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Beijing 100084, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A low-cost hybrid catalyst, carbon dots/copper oxide supported on mesoporous hydroxyapatite (CDs/CuO/mHA), was successfully prepared in this work. The resulting CDs/CuO/mHA showed excellent catalytic activity toward the reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) even under dark condition. It was demonstrated that the CuO phase underwent dynamic restructuring induced by the reactive environments and significantly boosted the electron transport, which fundamentally promoted the reduction of 4-NP. Introducing visible light illumination further resulted in an encouraging activity improvement owing to the acceleration of the restructuring of CuO by the photogenerated charges. In addition, CDs acting as electron donor, mHA providing porous structure and high specific surface area, as well as good interfacial bonding being the efficient charge transport channels, also played vital roles in improving the catalytic performance. The moles-normalized rate constant obtained for CDs/CuO/mHA was about 17 times higher than that obtained for CuO.

ce:section.ce:para: Developing high-performance catalysts has received extensive attention due to their great potential in resolving environmental pollution and energy crisis [1,2]. As a new rising star of carbon material family, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit promising catalytic properties because of their unique structural and physicochemical properties, such as tunable bandgap, various surface functional groups, excellent optical properties, and ability to act as electron donors or electron acceptors [3–9]. Furthermore, CDs are easy to synthesis, low cost, non-toxic, thus extremely advantageous for practical application [10]. Making full use of their merits, hybridizing CDs with other nanomaterials has attracted considerable research attention in recent years [11–15]. The obtained composites have shown remarkable improvement in activity.

CuO, an attractive class of transition metal oxides and p-type semiconductor, has been considered as an effective alternative to the noble metal catalysts due to its good redox property, appropriate band structure, earth-abundance in raw materials, low-cost and environmental friendliness [16–21]. But often, CuO is used to form heterostructure or hybrids with other materials to improve the catalytic performance. Research has shown that CuO is able to act as the electron trap or be reduced to Cu+ or Cu0 species in the catalytic process, which enhance charge transfer and provide effective active sites [22–25]. Specifically, the prior studies pointed out that the catalytic ability of CuO was strongly related to the reaction conditions and the interaction between CuO and other materials [16,26], which endows the CuO-contained composites with good designability and tunable catalytic properties. In view of such unique merits, the hybrid of CDs and CuO could be expected to achieve much enhanced activity.

Yet, it is known that nano-sized catalysts tend to form aggregation causing by their high surface energies, thereby inhibiting the activity [27]. An effective strategy for overcoming this issue is to immobilize the nanoparticles onto a support to realize both high catalytic activity and recyclability [28]. Given the large surface area and the capability to facilitate both charges transfer and reactants transport, the supports with mesoporous structure are particularly attractive for catalytic application [29–31]. Among the various catalytic supports investigated, hydroxyapatite (HA) is gaining increasing attention owing to its bifunctionality of basic and acidic sites, high adsorbability, good ion exchange property, and so forth [32–35].

Herein, we developed and constructed a new hybrid of carbon dots/copper oxide supported on mesoporous hydroxyapatite (CDs/CuO/mHA). In this structure, nano-sized CDs and CuO uniformly distributed on the surface of mHA, and an intimate contact between each component was achieved driven by the electrostatic forces and the interfacial reaction. Significantly, the synthesized CDs/CuO/mHA exhibited exceptional catalytic activity in the reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) even under dark condition. Such excellent activity was mainly attributed to the dynamic restructuring of CuO in response to the reactive environment. In addition, when visible light illumination was introduced, the activity of the composite was further enhanced owing to the boosted restructuring of CuO by the photogenerated charges. Besides the restructuring of CuO, CDs acting as electron donor, mHA providing higher surface area and facilitating mass transport, as well as the good interfacial bonding being the efficient charge transport channels, made considerable contribution in promoting the reduction of 4-NP. This study provided deep insight into the relationship between the dynamic restructuring and catalytic activity, and offered new enlightenments in designing hybrid catalysts and shaping their performances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs/CuO/mHA composite
ce:section.ce:para: In order to guarantee a good interface bonding between each component in the heterostructure, we proposed a method to fabricate the hybrid catalyst through first electrostatic/interfacial reaction assembly process and then hydrothermal treatment, as shown in Fig. 1 A. Briefly, CDs prepared using citric acid and sodium hydroxide as starting materials were chosen. On the one hand, the obtained CDs had negatively-charged surface, corroborated by their zeta potential in water (pH = 9.8) (Fig. S1). On the other hand, the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on CDs would be beneficial for their interaction with Cu2+ ions and mHA. Hydroxyapatite with mesoporous structure was selected as supports. The zeta potential of mHA in water was also negative (Fig. S1). When adding Cu(NO3)2 aqueous solution, Cu2+ ions could be attracted onto the surface of CDs and mHA driven by the electrostatic force. After subsequent hydrothermal treatment, the heterostructure was finally obtained. The details of preparation were given in the Supporting Information.

Fig. 1B shows the morphology and microstructure of CDs/CuO/mHA composite. mHA exhibited the same structure of short rods with mesopores distributed uniformly among particles as pure mHA (Fig. S2A). CDs were not observed in the low-magnification TEM image as a result of their small size of ca. 5 nm (Fig. S2B). Also, unlike pure CuO with large-sized irregular cubes (Fig. S2C), no large CuO particles were observed, implying the growth of CuO was suppressed and the desirable nano-sized CuO formed in the composite. Fig. 1C shows the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CDs/CuO/mHA taken from the edge of the composite. Three types of lattice fringes of 0.33 nm, 0.24 nm and 0.34 nm were observed (inset in Fig. 1C), corresponding to the facets of mHA (002), CuO (111) and CDs (002), respectively, which indicated that both CDs and CuO with similar size about 5 nm were successfully located on the surface of mHA and there was well-contacted interface between them. It is worth anticipating that such nano-sized CuO would favor the enhancement of activity. Also, the well-contacted interface could ensure the stability of the composite structure. Fig. 1D-H show the STEM and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental mapping images of CDs/CuO/mHA, revealing homogeneous distribution of C and Cu element on the surface of mHA. Note that the distribution of C almost covered the whole scanning area, which was attributed to the carbon film on the molybdenum grid. The morphology of CDs/CuO/mHA was further supported by the observation of control samples including CDs/mHA, CDs/CuO and CuO/mHA (Fig. S2D-I). Moreover, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were recorded to investigate the porous structure of CDs/CuO/mHA and shown in Fig. S3. Both mHA and CDs/CuO/mHA displayed type IV isotherm resulting from mesoporous structure. In comparison to pure mHA, the CDs/CuO/mHA composite showed an increased Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area from 48.76 m2 g−1 to 59.30 m2 g−1, further proving the incorporation of CDs and CuO nanoparticles so as to elevate the specific surface area.

The crystal structure and chemical states of as-prepared samples were evaluated by XRD, FTIR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 A, in the XRD pattern of CDs/CuO/mHA, the intensity of peak centered at about 2θ = 35.5° strengthened, and a new peak centered at 2θ = 38.7° was observed, which were attributed to the (002) and (111) plane of CuO (JCPDS, 080–1916), respectively. However, the existence of CDs could not be proved from the XRD pattern for two reasons: first, the peak at 26° corresponding to graphite overlaps with that of HA; second, the amount of CDs in the composite was about 2 wt% determined by inductively coupled plasma (ICP) result, which might be beyond the detection limit. Fig. 2B shows the FTIR spectra of the samples. For CDs/CuO/mHA, CO (1625 cm−1) and CC (1565 cm−1) stretching band were observed, and the peak intensity at 3300-3650 cm-1 assigned to OH stretching band increased, which evidenced the incorporation of CDs. Fig. 2C-E show the high resolution Ca2p, C1s and Cu2p XPS spectra. The XPS spectrum of Ca2p (Fig. 2C) exhibited typical characteristic peaks at 346.7 and 353.8 eV, corresponding to the Ca2p3/2 and Ca2p1/2, respectively. The deconvoluted C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2D) showed three peaks at 248.8, 286.0 and 288.9 eV, indicating the presence of CC/CC, CO and CO bonds, respectively. The Cu2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2E) revealed peaks around at 533.3 and 953.2 eV attributed to Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2, respectively, plus two satellite peaks at 942 and 962 eV, which indicated the chemical state of Cu2+. Additionally, the positions of Cu2p peaks in the composites shifted a little in comparison to pure CuO. And the shift in CDs/CuO/mHA was more obvious than those in binary systems. Generally, the shift of the peak positions was attributed to the changed electronic structure resulted from the interaction between the components [36]. Therefore, it cloud be speculated that there was a stronger interaction between CDs and CuO as well as mHA. Such interaction could lead to an intimate interfacial contact, which was consistent with the TEM results.

Furthermore, the optical absorption property of CDs/CuO/mHA was studied and shown in Fig. S4. It is observable that CDs/CuO/mHA exhibited an absorption peak at 270 nm, and a distinct absorbance in the visible light region from approximately 600–800 nm. The former was related to the π-π* transition of the CC from CDs [37], and the latter was ascribed to the absorption of CuO [38]. Taken together above, it can be concluded that CDs/CuO/mHA was successfully achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of CDs/CuO/mHA in the reduction of 4-NP
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic property of CDs/CuO/mHA was evaluated through the reduction of 4-NP by NaBH4 in ambient environment. Normally, the absorption peak of 4-NP is centered at 315–318 nm in neutral or acidic conditions [39]. After adding NaBH4, the color of 4-NP solution changes from light yellow to bright yellow, and the absorption peak shifts to 400 nm due to the formation of 4-nitrophenolate ions [28,40]. Although the reduction of 4-NP by NaBH4 meets the requirement of thermodynamics since the potential of NaBH4 (-1.33 V) is more negative than 4-NP (-0.76 V), the reaction can not proceed only in the presence of NaBH4, suggesting the reaction is restricted kinetically [41,42]. Therefore, catalysts are needed to promote the reduction of 4-NP. Owing to the proportion relationship between the intensity of the absorption peak and the concentration of 4-NP, the reduction process can be monitored via the UV–vis absorption spectra.

To our surprise, when we added CDs/CuO/mHA to the reaction solution of 4-NP and put it in dark to ensure the adsorption-desorption equilibrium, the color of the 4-NP was observed to turn light yellow. To elucidate the observation resulted from adsorption or reduction, we collected the solution and tested the UV–vis absorption. Excitingly, it was found that the absorption peak at 400 nm decreased and the absorption peak at 298 nm attributed to the reduction product 4-aminophenol (4-AP) appeared [39], which demonstrated the occurrence of the reduction of 4-NP. In order to better understand the activity of CDs/CuO/mHA, we systemically evaluated its catalytic performance under dark condition. Considering the fact that the reaction continued even in dark, to minimize the accumulative effects during the test process, each sample was only tested at one time interval instead of being tested continuously at different intervals. Moreover, all the reactions were performed under vigorous stirring.

Fig. 3 , S5 and S6 show the catalytic property of the prepared samples under dark condition. It is seen that the concentration of 4-NP was almost unchanged in the presence of CDs, mHA and CDs/mHA (Fig. S5A), indicating that CDs and mHA had no catalytic activity in the reduction of 4-NP. In contrast, the absorption peak of 4-NP at 400 nm decreased continuously, while a new absorption peak at 298 nm attributed to 4-AP gradually increased in the presence of CDs/CuO/mHA (Fig. 3A), CuO, CDs/CuO and CuO/mHA (Fig. S6). This indicated that CuO played a key role in the reduction of 4-NP. Fig. S5B shows the concentration variation of the 4-NP in the presence of catalyst but without NaBH4. Obviously, the concentration of 4-NP remained unchanged, demonstrating that NaBH4 as electron donor was also a prerequisite in the catalytic reduction of 4-NP.

As the amount of NaBH4 in the reduction of 4-NP was excess, the pseudo-first order kinetics could be employed to calculate the reaction rate [43]. The apparent reaction rate constant kapp is defined as:
(1)
d
C t
d t
= -
k
a p p
C t
where Ct is the concentration of 4-NP at time t. The correlation between ln(Ct/C0 ) and reaction time t could be fitted by an linear growth, and the kapp values can be obtained from the slops in Fig. 3B and S6. Since the proportion of CuO in these samples with the same mass used in the reduction of 4-NP was different, the intrinsic reaction rate constant knor values [44] were calculated by normalizing the kapp values by the number of moles of CuO and summarized in Table S1. The amount of CuO, calculated from the ICP results, was about 11 wt%, 93.1 wt%, and 8.3 wt% for CDs/CuO/mHA, CDs/CuO, and CuO/mHA. It is seen that CDs/CuO/mHA exhibited the superior catalytic activity compared with the control samples.

Arising from the results, two key issues were put forward: i) why CuO worked for the reduction of 4-NP and ii) why CDs/CuO/mHA was so much more active than the control samples. As is known, feasible electrons is the critical factor that enables the reduction of 4-NP. Therefore, the transport property of electron must be the essential point in understanding the exceptional catalytic activity of CDs/CuO/mHA.

During experimental process, we occasionally captured that little red color powders appeared in the reaction solution. This implied that the reduction of CuO to Cu2O or Cu occurred. To clarify the viewpoint, we collected CDs/CuO/mHA after the reduction reacted for 6 min and finished, respectively, and investigated the composition via XRD test. As shown in Fig. 4 A, weak peak of Cu2O at 2θ = 36.44° and Cu at 2θ = 44.30° were observed in the XRD patterns of CDs/CuO/mHA after reaction, indicating the reduction of CuO during the catalytic reduction of 4-NP. Based on the references which demonstrated that the reduced CuxO or Cu was the root reason for promoting many chemical reactions [25,26], it seems plausible that Cu2O or Cu might contribute to the reduction of 4-NP. To verify the hypothesis, the following experiments were performed. We prepared Cu2O using the procedure reported in reference [45]. XRD result (Fig. 4B) revealed Cu2O was successfully obtained without impurity. However, the red Cu2O powder immediately turned black when being added to the reaction solution due to the oxidation (inset in Fig. 4B). Then, Cu was synthesized by reducing CuO using NaBH4 in ambient environment. Nevertheless, besides Cu phase, CuxO still remained in the final product (Fig. S7), despite the amount of NaBH4 and the reaction time were much larger and longer than that used in the reduction of 4-NP. The above findings revealed that Cu2O was highly instable and rapidly oxidized in the reaction solution. In this instance, the reduction of CuO to Cu was suppressed despite the presence of NaBH4. This was corroborated by the XRD result (Fig. 4A), where only weak peak from Cu2O and Cu was observed owing to the few amounts of them in the composite. Therefore, it could be established that both Cu2O and Cu phase were not the dominant contributors to the catalytic reduction of 4-NP even though they possessed the activity. But importantly, these findings signified an interesting phenomenon that CuO underwent a dynamic and drastic restructuring in response to the reactive environment.

As is known, the restructuring accompanies by a process of electron transport. Taking into account the essentiality of the kinetic process of electron transfer in the catalytic reduction of 4-NP and the above findings, we proposed it was the dynamic restructuring of CuO driven by the reactive environment that fundamentally led to the reduction of 4-NP. It could be described as: electron first transferred from NaBH4 to CuO and reduced CuO to Cu2O, then quickly released during the oxidation of Cu2O, and then the released electron transferred from catalyst to 4-NP. Furthermore, the strong bonding between CuxO and nitro groups of 4-NP could make N–O bond be much easier to cleave [44], also providing a potential to promote the reduction of 4-NP. Meanwhile, as revealed by the TEM result, the size of CuO in the composite was decreased significantly compared with pure CuO, which was also favorable for enhancing the activity because such nano-sized CuO with higher surface areas can facilitate the charge transfer on CuO surface thus accelerate the restructuring of CuO.

The reason that the incorporation of CDs and mHA significantly improved the catalytic activity could be understood as follows. For CDs, their rich π structure made 4-NP easily adsorbed on the surface of CDs through π-π stacking interactions since 4-NP also possesses π structure, which could result in a higher concentration of 4-NP near CuO and give rise to an improved reaction rate. For the key role of mHA, an appealing basis has been provided by the results from morphology observation and N2 adsorption-desorption experiments (Fig. S2 and S3), which indicated that HA possessed mesoporous structure and large surface area. These distinctive features played a vital role in suppressing the growth and aggregation of CuO (Fig. 1 and S2), thus creating more active sites. Moreover, mHA can facilitate the mass transport, which was beneficial for improving the reaction rate. In addition, as discussed above, there was a good interfacial bonding between the three components, which could facilitate the electron interaction and transfer [24,40]. For these reasons, CDs/CuO/mHA exhibited superior catalytic property in comparison to CuO, CDs/CuO and CuO/mHA.

To better understand the catalytic behavior of CDs/CuO/mHA, the reduction process was also investigated under visible light illumination. As shown in Fig. 5 , S8 and S9, the reaction rates of these samples were remarkably improved after the introduction of illumination. Moreover, it is seen that the catalytic activity increased with the increase of light intensity. When illuminated with the visible light at 400 mW/m−2, the knor obtained for CDs/CuO/mHA reached the maximum value of 1.847 × 104 min-1 mol-1, which was about 17 times higher than that obtained for CuO.

Under illumination, the photogenerated electrons of both CDs and CuO could be activated, which might participate the reduction of 4-NP. However, the study showed that no reduction of 4-NP was observed in the presence of catalysts and visible light illumination but without NaBH4 (Fig. S5B), suggesting that the photogenerated electrons were not able to lead to the reduction of 4-NP. The possible reasons for the improvement of activity resulted from illumination are discussed as follows.

First, the fast photooxdoxidation of Cu2O driven by the photogenerated h+ [45] could release significant amount of e− for reduction so as to improve the reaction rate. As verification to this viewpoint, the reduction of 4-NP over CDs/CuO/mHA in the presence of ethanol as h+ scavenger was performed and shown in Fig. 6 A. Noticeably, CDs/CuO/mHA displayed a reduced activity in the presence of ethanol when compared to sample in a non-scavenging system. This was credited to the presence of ethanol that consumed the photogenerated h+ and suppressed the photooxidation of Cu2O which reduced the amount of e−, thus leading to a decreased catalytic activity.

Second, the photocatalytic activity of the CDs-contained composite was dependent on the electron donating or accepting property of CDs [9,10]. The photoluminescence (PL) of CDs was investigated in the presence of acceptor 2,4-dinitrotoluene and donor N,N-diethylaninline. The PL intensity was quenched by acceptor 2,4-dinitrotoluene, while the PL intensity increased slightly in donor N,N-diethylaniline solution (Fig. 6B), indicating that CDs used in this work acted as electron donor. In this case, when introducing illumination, the photogenerated electrons of CDs would transfer to CuO and lead to a higher electron density near CuO, which promoted the reduction of CuO. Correspondingly, the restructuring of CuO was accelerated that boosted the electron transport, thus enhancing the reaction rate. To gain if the electron-donating ability of CDs affected its stability, the used CDs/CuO/mHA was characterized by ICP and FTIR. Desirably, the content of CDs (1.98%) and CuO (11.1%) after reaction was almost unchanged. And the used CDs/CuO/mHA exhibited similar FTIR spectra with that of CDs/CuO/mHA before reaction (Fig. S10), revealing the similar structure. This demonstrated the good stability of the composite.

In addition, another very important factor for activity was related to the charges transfer occurred at the interfaces. The charge-transfer behaviors in the composites were investigated by measuring the transient photocurrent responses and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As shown in Fig. 6C, the composites showed better photoelectric responses under visible light illumination (λ > 420 nm) in comparison to CuO, indicating more efficient separation and transfer of the photogenerated charges. The phenomenon that the photocurrent density of CDs/CuO/mHA was lower than that of CuO/CDs could be explained as the lower content of CuO in CDs/CuO/mHA resulted in a lower optical utilization that yielded less amount of photogenerated charges. Interestingly, for CDs/CuO/mHA and CuO/CDs, it can be seen that the current density first increased quickly and then slowly increased to a constant value. When the light was off, there was an instead tail in reaching the background value. But this phenomenon did not present in CuO and CuO/mHA. This effect can be attributed to the introduction of CDs that led to photogenerated electrons with sufficient energy being trapped by CuO, giving rise to the delay of photocurrent response [25]. Fig. 6D shows the EIS Nyquist plots of the as prepared samples. It was observed that the arc radius of CDs/CuO was the smallest, followed by CDs/CuO/mHA, associating to the obtained photocurrent responses. Usually, the smaller the arc radius, the lower the resistance, the faster the interfacial charge transfer [46]. These results fully manifested the fast interfacial charge transfer in the composites, thus boosting the catalytic performance. Such good charge transport ability further proved the intimate interfacial contact between the components since only good interfacial bonding could act as efficient transport channels of photogenerated charges [40].

Furthermore, the phenomenon that the higher activity obtained at intensity of 400 mW/cm2 than that obtained at intensity of 100 mW/cm2 could be understood by the fact that more photons were produced when using higher light intensity. Such more photogenerated charges could promote the reconstructing of CuO, thus enhancing the reduction of 4-NP. It is worthwhile to mention that the increase of light intensity also led to an increase of temperature of the reaction solution (Fig. S11A). In accordance with the Arrhenius equation [27]:
(2)
k = A
e
- E a
R T
where k is kinetic constant, Ea is the activation energy, A is the frequency factor and T is the reaction temperature, if the activation energy is a constant, the k should be proportional to the temperature [27,47]. To differentiate the contributions of more photogenerated charges and higher temperature caused by higher light intensity, the reduction of 4-NP over CDs/CuO/mHA under controlled temperature at 20 °C ± 1 °C was carried out. It can be seen from Fig. S11B that CDs/CuO/mHA showed very close catalytic activity with that obtained under non-controlled temperature condition, indicating that the photogenerated charges had a higher-impact than the temperature. Our previous work has also found that the activity of CDs/Ag3PO4 heterostructure did not increase with increasing the temperature, and the optimal reaction temperature was about 25 °C [12]. This non-positive relation between the reaction rate and the reaction temperature was likely because the reaction rate was usually influenced by various factors.

Apart from the role of light intensity, wavelength λ is another important aspect to be considered [48–50]. The wavelength dependence of activity of CDs/CuO/mHA was investigated at a fixed light intensity of 100 mW/cm2. Fig. 7 A shows the conversion rate after the same reaction time of 6 min. It is seen that the conversion rate increased with the increase of wavelength. This was partially associated with the optical absorption property. When the sample was illuminated with the wavelength of 450 nm, although both CDs and CuO could be excited, only small amounts of photogenerated electrons were produced since the excitation wavelengths was not in the optimal region of CuO absorption. For the wavelength of 550 nm, it corresponded to the absorption of Cu2O [45]. As discussed above, Cu2O as transient phase generated during the reaction process, which could lead to the increase of the optical absorption at a certain extent so as to improve the reaction rate. Further increasing the wavelength to 650 nm, it was in the region of CuO absorption that led to the highest optical absorption which produced more photocharges so as to significantly promote the reaction. The schematic mechanism of catalytic reduction of 4-NP by CDs/CuO/mHA under dark and illumination conditions is shown in Fig. 8 .

Finally, the reusability of CDs/CuO/mHA was investigated. Although traces of Cu2O and Cu remained in the composite, they could be easily oxidized in aerobic condition [26]. The collected sample after reaction was reheated at low temperature 40 °C and performed the recycling test under illumination with visible light at 100 mW/cm2. It is seen from Fig. 7B that there were negligible drops in the activity, 90% 4-NP conversion was still maintained after five successive runs, further confirming the good stability of CDs/CuO/mHA.

In this study a new carbon dots/copper oxide supported on mesoporous hydroxyapatite (CDs/CuO/mHA) nanocomposite has been achieved. The obtained CDs/CuO/mHA exhibited good catalytic activity in the reduction of 4-NP under dark condition. It was found that the CuO phase underwent dynamic restructuring driven by the reactive environment. This dynamic restructuring significantly boosted the kinetic process of electron transfer on the surface of catalysts and thus promoted the reduction of 4-NP. When visible light illumination was introduced, the activity was further improved. This could be attributed to the photogenerated charges that accelerated the restructuring of CuO so as to improve the catalytic performance. Moreover, the exceptional activity of CDs/CuO/mHA was also associated with the CDs as electron donor, mHA with porous structure and high specific surface area, and well-contacted interface. These factors synergistically enhanced the activity. As a result, the moles-normalized rate constant obtained for CDs/CuO/mHA was about 17-fold higher than that obtained for CuO.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51502270, U1510125, 51602292, 21703209), Shanxi Province Science Foundation for Youths and International Cooperation (201801D221085, 201803D421091), Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi (OIT), Program for the Young Academic Leaders and Scientific Innovation Team of North University of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2019.118299.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
